--- SOURCE 3: Reverse osmosis - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reverse_osmosis

SUMMARY:
Reverse osmosis (RO) is a water purification process that uses a semi-permeable membrane to separate water molecules from other substances.

FULL CONTENT:

![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

## Contents

# Reverse osmosis

![Page semi-protected](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/1b/Semi-protection-shackle.svg/20px-Semi-protection-shackle.svg.png)

| [Water desalination](/wiki/Desalination "Desalination")   --- |
| --- |
| Methods |
| * Distillation   + [Multi-stage flash distillation](/wiki/Multi-stage_flash_distillation "Multi-stage flash distillation") (MSF)   + [Multiple-effect distillation](/wiki/Multiple-effect_distillation "Multiple-effect distillation") (MED)   + [Vapor-compression](/wiki/Vapor-compression_desalination "Vapor-compression desalination") (VC) * [Ion exchange](/wiki/Ion_exchange "Ion exchange") * [Membrane processes](/wiki/Membrane_technology "Membrane technology")   + [Electrodialysis reversal](/wiki/Electrodialysis_reversal "Electrodialysis reversal") (EDR)   + Reverse osmosis (RO)   + [Nanofiltration](/wiki/Nanofiltration "Nanofiltration") (NF)   + [Membrane distillation](/wiki/Membrane_distillation "Membrane distillation") (MD)   + [Forward osmosis](/wiki/Forward_osmosis "Forward osmosis") (FO) * [Freezing desalination](/wiki/Freezing_desalination "Freezing desalination") * [Geothermal desalination](/wiki/Geothermal_desalination "Geothermal desalination") * [Solar desalination](/wiki/Solar_desalination "Solar desalination")   + [Solar humidification](/wiki/Solar_humidification "Solar humidification")–dehumidification (HDH)   + [Multiple-effect humidification](/wiki/Multiple-effect_humidification "Multiple-effect humidification") (MEH)   + [Seawater greenhouse](/wiki/Seawater_greenhouse "Seawater greenhouse") * [Methane hydrate](/wiki/Methane_hydrate "Methane hydrate") crystallization * High grade [water recycling](/wiki/Water_recycling "Water recycling") * [Wave-powered desalination](/wiki/Wave-powered_desalination "Wave-powered desalination") |

**Reverse [osmosis](/wiki/Osmosis "Osmosis")** (**RO**) is a [water purification](/wiki/Water_purification "Water purification") process that uses a [semi-permeable membrane](/wiki/Partially_permeable_membrane "Partially permeable membrane") to separate water molecules from other substances. RO applies pressure to overcome [osmotic pressure](/wiki/Osmotic_pressure "Osmotic pressure") that favors even distributions. RO can remove dissolved or suspended [chemical species](/wiki/Chemical_species "Chemical species") as well as biological substances (principally [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria")), and is used in industrial processes and the production of [potable water](/wiki/Potable_water "Potable water").

RO retains the [solute](/wiki/Solute "Solute") on the pressurized side of the membrane and the purified [solvent](/wiki/Solvent "Solvent") passes to the other side. The relative sizes of the various molecules determines what passes through. "Selective" membranes reject large molecules, while accepting smaller molecules (such as solvent molecules, e.g., water).[[1]](#cite_note-WarsingerBatch-1)

Reverse osmosis is most commonly known for its use in drinking [water purification](/wiki/Water_purification "Water purification") from [seawater](/wiki/Seawater "Seawater"), removing the salt and other [effluent](/wiki/Effluent "Effluent") materials from the water molecules.[[2]](#cite_note-2) As of 2013 the world's largest RO desalination plant was in [Sorek, Israel](/wiki/Nahal_Sorek "Nahal Sorek"), outputting 624 thousand cubic metres per day (165 million US gallons per day).[[3]](#cite_note-3) RO systems for private use are also available for purifying municipal tap water or pre-treated well water.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

A process of osmosis through semi-permeable membranes was first observed in 1748 by [Jean-Antoine Nollet](/wiki/Jean-Antoine_Nollet "Jean-Antoine Nollet"). For the following 200 years, osmosis was only a laboratory phenomenon. In 1950, the [University of California at Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California_at_Los_Angeles "University of California at Los Angeles") (UCLA) first investigated osmotic [desalination](/wiki/Desalination "Desalination"). Researchers at both UCLA and [University of Florida](/wiki/University_of_Florida "University of Florida") desalinated seawater in the mid-1950s, but the [flux](/wiki/Flux "Flux") was too low to be commercially viable.[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Sidney Loeb](/wiki/Sidney_Loeb "Sidney Loeb") at UCLA and Srinivasa Sourirajan[[6]](#cite_note-6) at the [National Research Council of Canada](/wiki/National_Research_Council_of_Canada "National Research Council of Canada"), Ottawa, found techniques for making asymmetric membranes characterized by an effectively thin "skin" layer supported atop a highly porous and much thicker substrate region. John Cadotte, of [Filmtec corporation](/wiki/Filmtec_corporation "Filmtec corporation"), discovered that membranes with particularly high flux and low salt passage could be made by [interfacial polymerization](/wiki/Interfacial_polymerization "Interfacial polymerization") of [*m*-phenylene diamine](/wiki/M-Phenylenediamine "M-Phenylenediamine") and trimesoyl chloride. Cadotte's patent on this process[[7]](#cite_note-7) was the subject of litigation and expired. Almost all commercial RO membrane is now made by this method. By 2019, approximately 16,000 [desalination](/wiki/Desalination "Desalination") plants operated around the world, producing around 95 million cubic metres per day (25 billion US gallons per day). Around half of this capacity was in the Middle East and North Africa region.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Northcapecoral-RO.jpg/250px-Northcapecoral-RO.jpg)

In 1977 [Cape Coral](/wiki/Cape_Coral "Cape Coral"), Florida became the first US municipality to use RO at scale, with an initial operating capacity of 11.35 million liters (3 million US gal) per day. By 1985, rapid growth led the city to operate the world's largest low-pressure RO plant, producing 56.8 million liters (15 million US gal) per day (MGD).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Osmosis

In (forward) [osmosis](/wiki/Osmosis "Osmosis"), the solvent moves from an area of low solute concentration (high [water potential](/wiki/Water_potential "Water potential")), through a membrane, to an area of high solute concentration (low water potential). The driving force for the movement of the solvent is the reduction in the [Gibbs free energy](/wiki/Gibbs_free_energy "Gibbs free energy") of the system in which the difference in solvent concentration between the sides of a membrane is reduced. This is called osmotic pressure. It reduces as the solvent moves into the more concentrated solution. Applying an external pressure to reverse the natural flow of pure solvent, thus, is reverse osmosis. The process is similar to other membrane technology applications.

RO differs from [filtration](/wiki/Filtration "Filtration") in that the mechanism of fluid flow is reversed, as the solvent crosses membrane, leaving the solute behind. The predominant removal mechanism in membrane filtration is straining, or size exclusion, where the pores are 0.01 [micrometers](/wiki/Micrometre "Micrometre") or larger, so the process can theoretically achieve perfect efficiency regardless of parameters such as the solution's pressure and concentration. RO instead involves solvent [diffusion](/wiki/Diffusion "Diffusion") across a membrane that is either nonporous or uses [nanofiltration](/wiki/Nanofiltration "Nanofiltration") with pores 0.001 micrometers in size. The predominant removal mechanism is from differences in [solubility](/wiki/Solubility "Solubility") or [diffusivity](/wiki/Diffusivity_(biology) "Diffusivity (biology)"), and the process is dependent on [pressure](/wiki/Pressure "Pressure"), solute concentration, and other conditions.[[10]](#cite_note-water-10)

RO requires pressure between 2–17 [bar](/wiki/Bar_(unit) "Bar (unit)") (30–250 [psi](/wiki/Pound-force_per_square_inch "Pound-force per square inch")) for fresh and brackish water, and 40–82 bar (600–1200 psi) for seawater. Seawater has around 27 bar (390 psi)[[11]](#cite_note-11) natural [osmotic pressure](/wiki/Osmotic_pressure "Osmotic pressure") that must be overcome. As for their energy consumption, seawater RO systems typically require 2.9-5.5 kWh/m3,[[12]](#cite_note-a897-12) although state-of-the-art systems are around 2.3 kWh/m3.[[13]](#cite_note-a862-13)

Membrane pore sizes vary from 0.1 to 5,000 nm. Particle filtration removes particles of 1 [μm](/wiki/Micrometre "Micrometre") or larger. [Microfiltration](/wiki/Microfiltration "Microfiltration") removes particles of 50 nm or larger. [Ultrafiltration](/wiki/Ultrafiltration "Ultrafiltration") removes particles of roughly 3 nm or larger. Nanofiltration removes particles of 1 nm or larger. RO is in the final category of membrane filtration, hyperfiltration, and removes particles larger than ~0.2 nm.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Fresh water applications

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Yen_Sun_Technology_YS-8103RWT_20201101.jpg/250px-Yen_Sun_Technology_YS-8103RWT_20201101.jpg)

### Drinking water purification

Around the world, household [drinking water](/wiki/Drinking_water "Drinking water") [purification](/wiki/Water_purification "Water purification") systems, including an RO step, are commonly used for improving water for drinking and cooking.

Such systems typically include these steps:

In some systems, the carbon prefilter is replaced by a [cellulose triacetate](/wiki/Cellulose_triacetate "Cellulose triacetate") (CTA) membrane. CTA is a paper by-product membrane bonded to a synthetic layer that allows contact with chlorine in the water. These require a small amount of chlorine in the water source to prevent bacteria from forming on it. The typical rejection rate for CTA membranes is 85–95%.

The cellulose triacetate membrane rots unless protected by [chlorinated water](/wiki/Water_chlorination "Water chlorination"), while the thin-film composite membrane breaks down in the presence of chlorine. The [thin-film composite](/wiki/Thin-film_composite "Thin-film composite") (TFC) membrane is made of synthetic material, and requires the chlorine to be removed before the water enters the membrane. To protect the TFC membrane elements from chlorine damage, [carbon filters](/wiki/Carbon_filter "Carbon filter") are used as pre-treatment. TFC membranes have a higher rejection rate of 95–98% and a longer life than CTA membranes.

To work effectively, the water feeding to these units should be under pressure (typically 280 kPa (40 psi) or greater).[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Though Portable RO Water Purifiers are commercially available and extensively used in areas lacking cleaning potable water, in Europe such processing of natural [mineral water](/wiki/Mineral_water "Mineral water") (as defined by a European directive)[[16]](#cite_note-16) is not allowed. In practice, a fraction of the living bacteria pass through RO through membrane imperfections or bypass the membrane entirely through leaks in seals.

#### Solar-powered RO

A [solar-powered desalination unit](/wiki/Solar-powered_desalination_unit "Solar-powered desalination unit") produces [potable water](/wiki/Potable_water "Potable water") from [saline water](/wiki/Saline_water "Saline water") by using a [photovoltaic](/wiki/Photovoltaic "Photovoltaic") system to supply the energy. Solar power works well for water purification in settings lacking grid electricity and can reduce operating costs and [greenhouse emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_emissions "Greenhouse emissions"). For example, a solar-powered desalination unit designed passed tests in [Australia's](/wiki/Australia%27s "Australia's") [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory").[[17]](#cite_note-uow-17)

Sunlight's intermittent nature makes output prediction difficult without an energy storage capability. However batteries or [thermal energy storage](/wiki/Thermal_energy_storage "Thermal energy storage") systems can provide power when the sun does not.[[18]](#cite_note-TES-18)

#### Military

Larger scale reverse osmosis water purification units (ROWPU) exist for military use. These have been adopted by the [United States armed forces](/wiki/United_States_armed_forces "United States armed forces") and the [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces"). Some models are [containerized](/wiki/Containerization "Containerization"), some are trailers, and some are themselves vehicles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The water is treated with a [polymer](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") to initiate [coagulation](/wiki/Coagulation "Coagulation"). Next, it is run through a multi-media filter where it undergoes primary treatment, removing [turbidity](/wiki/Turbidity "Turbidity"). It is then pumped through a cartridge filter which is usually spiral-wound cotton. This process strips any particles larger than 5 [μm](/wiki/Micrometre "Micrometre") and eliminates almost all turbidity.

The clarified water is then fed through a high-pressure piston pump into a series of RO vessels. 90.00–99.98% of the raw water's [total dissolved solids](/wiki/Total_dissolved_solids "Total dissolved solids") are removed and military standards require that the result have no more than 1000–1500 [parts per million](/wiki/Parts_per_million "Parts per million") by measure of [electrical conductivity](/wiki/Electrical_conductivity "Electrical conductivity"). It is then disinfected with [chlorine](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Water and wastewater purification

RO-purified rainwater collected from storm drains is used for landscape irrigation and industrial cooling in Los Angeles and other cities.

In industry, RO removes minerals from [boiler water](/wiki/Boiler_water "Boiler water") at [power plants](/wiki/Power_plant "Power plant").[[19]](#cite_note-19) The water is [distilled](/wiki/Distilled "Distilled") multiple times to ensure that it does not leave deposits on the machinery or cause corrosion.

RO is used to clean effluent and [brackish groundwater](/wiki/Brackish_water "Brackish water"). The effluent in larger volumes (more than 500 m3/day) is treated in a [water treatment plant](/wiki/Water_treatment_plant "Water treatment plant") first, and then the effluent runs through RO. This hybrid process reduces treatment cost significantly and lengthens membrane life.

RO can be used for the production of [deionized water](/wiki/Deionized_water "Deionized water").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 2002, Singapore announced that a process named [NEWater](/wiki/NEWater "NEWater") would be a significant part of its water plans. RO would be used to treat wastewater before discharging the effluent into reservoirs.

### Food industry

Reverse osmosis is a more economical way to concentrate liquids (such as fruit juices) than conventional heat-treatment. Concentration of orange and tomato juice has advantages including a lower operating cost and the ability to avoid heat-treatment, which makes it suitable for heat-sensitive substances such as [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein") and [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme").

RO is used in the dairy industry to produce [whey protein](/wiki/Whey_protein "Whey protein") powders and concentrate milk. The [whey](/wiki/Whey "Whey") (liquid remaining after cheese manufacture) is concentrated with RO from 6% solids to 10–20% solids before [ultrafiltration](/wiki/Ultrafiltration "Ultrafiltration") processing. The retentate can then be used to make whey powders, including [whey protein isolate](/wiki/Whey_protein_isolate "Whey protein isolate"). Additionally, the permeate, which contains [lactose](/wiki/Lactose "Lactose"), is concentrated by RO from 5% solids to 18–total solids to reduce crystallization and drying costs.

Although RO was once avoided in the wine industry, it is now widespread. An estimated 60 RO machines were in use in [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux"), France, in 2002. Known users include many of elite firms, such as [Château Léoville-Las Cases](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_L%C3%A9oville-Las_Cases "Château Léoville-Las Cases").

### Maple syrup production

In 1946, some [maple syrup](/wiki/Maple_syrup "Maple syrup") producers started using RO to remove water from [sap](/wiki/Plant_sap "Plant sap") before boiling the sap to [syrup](/wiki/Syrup "Syrup"). RO allows about 75–90% of the water to be removed, reducing energy consumption and exposure of the syrup to high temperatures.

### Low-alcohol beer

When beer at typical concentration is subjected to reverse osmosis, both water and alcohol pass across the membrane more readily than other components, leaving a "beer concentrate". The concentrate is then diluted with fresh water to restore the non-volatile components to their original intensity.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Hydrogen production

For small-scale [hydrogen production](/wiki/Hydrogen_production "Hydrogen production"), RO is sometimes used to prevent formation of mineral deposits on the surface of [electrodes](/wiki/Electrode "Electrode").

### Aquariums

Many [reef aquarium](/wiki/Reef_aquarium "Reef aquarium") keepers use RO systems to make fish-friendly seawater. Ordinary tap water can contain excessive [chlorine](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine"), [chloramines](/wiki/Chloramines "Chloramines"), [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), [nitrates](/wiki/Nitrate "Nitrate"), [nitrites](/wiki/Nitrite "Nitrite"), [phosphates](/wiki/Phosphate "Phosphate"), [silicates](/wiki/Silicate "Silicate"), or other chemicals detrimental to marine organisms. Contaminants such as [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") and phosphates can lead to unwanted algae growth. An effective combination of both RO and [deionization](/wiki/Deionization "Deionization") is popular among reef aquarium keepers, and is preferred above other water purification processes due to the low cost of ownership and operating costs. Where [chlorine](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine") and [chloramines](/wiki/Chloramine "Chloramine") are found in the water, carbon filtration is needed before RO, as common residential membranes do not address these compounds.

Freshwater aquarists also use RO to duplicate the soft waters found in many tropical waters. While many tropical fish can survive in treated tap water, breeding can be impossible. Many aquatic shops sell containers of RO water for this purpose.

### Window cleaning

An increasingly popular method of cleaning windows is the "water-fed pole" system. Instead of washing windows with conventional detergent, they are scrubbed with purified water, typically containing less than 10 ppm dissolved solids, using a brush on the end of a pole wielded from ground level. RO is commonly used to purify the water.

## Landfill leachate purification

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Reverse_osmosis "Special:EditPage/Reverse osmosis") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(May 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)

Treatment with RO is limited, resulting in low recoveries on high concentration (measured with [electrical conductivity](/wiki/Electrical_conductivity "Electrical conductivity")) and membrane fouling. RO applicability is limited by conductivity, organics, and scaling inorganic elements such as CaSO4, Si, Fe and Ba. Low organic scaling can use two different technologies: spiral wound membrane, and (for high organic scaling, high conductivity and higher pressure (up to 90 bars)), disc tube modules with RO membranes can be used. Disc tube modules were redesigned for landfill [leachate](/wiki/Leachate "Leachate") purification that is usually contaminated with organic material. Due to the cross-flow, it is given a flow booster pump that recirculates the flow over the membrane between 1.5 and 3 times before it is released as a concentrate. High velocity protects against [membrane scaling](/wiki/Membrane_scaling "Membrane scaling") and allows membrane cleaning.

### Power consumption for a disc tube module system

![Disc tube module and Spiral wound module](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Disc_tube_module_and_Spiral_wound_module.png/250px-Disc_tube_module_and_Spiral_wound_module.png)

| Energy consumption per m3 leachate | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| name of module | 1-stage up to 75 bar | 2-stage up to 75 bar | 3-stage up to 120 bar |
| disc tube module | 6.1–8.1 kWh/m3 | 8.1–9.8 kWh/m3 | 11.2–14.3 kWh/m3 |

## Desalination

Areas that have limited surface water or groundwater may choose to [desalinate](/wiki/Desalination "Desalination"). RO is an increasingly common method, because of its relatively low energy consumption.[[22]](#cite_note-WarsingerEntropy-22)

Energy consumption is around 3 kWh/m3 (11,000 J/L), with the development of more efficient [energy recovery](/wiki/Energy_recovery "Energy recovery") devices and improved membrane materials. According to the [International Desalination Association](/wiki/International_Desalination_Association "International Desalination Association"), for 2011, RO was used in 66% of installed desalination capacity (0.0445 of 0.0674 km3/day), and nearly all new plants.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Other plants use thermal distillation methods: [multiple-effect distillation](/wiki/Multiple-effect_distillation "Multiple-effect distillation"), and [multi-stage flash](/wiki/Multi-Stage_Flash "Multi-Stage Flash").

Sea-water RO (SWRO) desalination requires around 3 kWh/m3, much higher than those required for other forms of water supply, including RO treatment of wastewater, at 0.1 to 1 kWh/m3. Up to 50% of the seawater input can be recovered as fresh water, though lower recovery rates may reduce membrane fouling and energy consumption.

Brackish water reverse osmosis (BWRO) is the desalination of water with less salt than seawater, usually from river estuaries or saline wells. The process is substantially the same as SWRO, but requires lower pressures and less energy.[[1]](#cite_note-WarsingerBatch-1) Up to 80% of the feed water input can be recovered as fresh water, depending on feed salinity.

The [Ashkelon](/wiki/Ashkelon "Ashkelon") desalination plant in Israel is the world's largest.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The typical single-pass SWRO system consists of:

### Pretreatment

Pretreatment is important when working nanofiltration membranes due to their spiral-wound design. The material is engineered to allow one-way flow. The design does not allow for backpulsing with water or air agitation to scour its surface and remove accumulated solids. Since material cannot be removed from the membrane surface, it is susceptible to [fouling](/wiki/Fouling "Fouling") (loss of production capacity). Therefore, pretreatment is a necessity for any RO or nanofiltration system. Pretreatment has four major components:

### High pressure pump

The high pressure [pump](/wiki/Pump "Pump") pushes water through the membrane. Typical pressures for [brackish water](/wiki/Brackish_water "Brackish water") range from 1.6 to 2.6 MPa (225 to 376 psi). In the case of seawater, they range from 5.5 to 8 MPa (800 to 1,180 psi). This requires substantial energy. Where energy recovery is used, part of the high pressure pump's work is done by the energy recovery device, reducing energy inputs.

### Membrane assembly

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Reverse_osmosis_membrane_element_layers.jpg/250px-Reverse_osmosis_membrane_element_layers.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Cutaway_of_a_16%22_reverse_osmosis_tube.jpg/250px-Cutaway_of_a_16%22_reverse_osmosis_tube.jpg)

The membrane assembly consists of a pressure vessel with a membrane that allows feedwater to be pushed against it. The membrane must be strong enough to withstand the pressure. RO membranes are made in a variety of configurations. The two most common are spiral-wound and [hollow-fiber](/wiki/Hollow_fiber_membrane "Hollow fiber membrane").

Only part of the water pumped onto the membrane passes through. The left-behind "concentrate" passes along the saline side of the membrane and flushes away the salt and other remnants. The percentage of desalinated water is the "recovery ratio". This varies with salinity and system design parameters: typically 20% for small seawater systems, 40% – 50% for larger seawater systems, and 80% – 85% for brackish water. The concentrate flow is typically 3 bar/50 psi less than the feed pressure, and thus retains much of the input energy.

The desalinated water purity is a function of the feed water salinity, membrane selection and recovery ratio. To achieve higher purity a second pass can be added which generally requires another pumping cycle. Purity expressed as [total dissolved solids](/wiki/Total_dissolved_solids "Total dissolved solids") typically varies from 100 to 400 parts per million (ppm or mg/litre) on a seawater feed. A level of 500 ppm is generally the upper limit for drinking water, while the [US Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration") classifies [mineral water](/wiki/Mineral_water "Mineral water") as water containing at least 250 ppm.

### Energy recovery

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/ReverseOsmosis_with_PressureExchanger.svg/330px-ReverseOsmosis_with_PressureExchanger.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Reverse_Osmosis_with_Pressure_Recovery_Pump.jpg/330px-Reverse_Osmosis_with_Pressure_Recovery_Pump.jpg)

Energy recovery can reduce energy consumption by 50% or more. Much of the input energy can be recovered from the concentrate flow, and the increasing efficiency of energy recovery devices greatly reduces energy requirements. Devices used, in order of invention, are:

### Remineralisation and pH adjustment

The desalinated water is stabilized to protect downstream pipelines and storage, usually by adding [lime](/wiki/Lime_(material) "Lime (material)") or [caustic soda](/wiki/Sodium_hydroxide "Sodium hydroxide") to prevent corrosion of concrete-lined surfaces. Liming material is used to adjust pH between 6.8 and 8.1 to meet the potable water specifications, primarily for effective disinfection and for corrosion control. Remineralisation may be needed to replace minerals removed from the water by desalination, although this process has proved to be costly and inconvenient in order to meet mineral demand by humans and plants as found in typical freshwater. For instance water from Israel's national water carrier typically contains dissolved magnesium levels of 20 to 25 mg/liter, while water from the [Ashkelon](/wiki/Ashkelon "Ashkelon") plant has no magnesium. Ashkelon water created [magnesium-deficiency](/wiki/Magnesium_deficiency "Magnesium deficiency") symptoms in crops, including tomatoes, basil, and flowers, and had to be remedied by fertilization. Israeli drinking water standards require a minimum calcium level of 20 mg/liter. Askelon's post-desalination treatment uses sulfuric acid to dissolve calcite (limestone), resulting in calcium concentrations of 40 to 46 mg/liter, lower than the 45 to 60 mg/liter found in typical Israeli fresh water.

### Disinfection

Post-treatment disinfection provides secondary protection against compromised membranes and downstream problems. Disinfection by means of [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet_radiation "Ultraviolet radiation") (UV) lamps (sometimes called germicidal or bactericidal) may be employed to sterilize pathogens that evade the RO process. [Chlorination](/wiki/Water_chlorination "Water chlorination") or [chloramination](/wiki/Chloramination "Chloramination") (chlorine and ammonia) protects against pathogens that may have lodged in the distribution system downstream.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Disadvantages

Large-scale industrial/municipal systems recover typically 75% to 80% of the feed water, or as high as 90%, because they can generate the required higher pressure.

### Wastewater

Household RO units use a lot of water because they have low back pressure. Household RO water purifiers typically produce one liter of usable water and 3-25 liters of [wastewater](/wiki/Wastewater "Wastewater").[[34]](#cite_note-34) The remainder is discharged, usually into the drain. Because wastewater carries the rejected contaminants, recovering this water is not practical for household systems. Wastewater is typically delivered to house drains. #### Breadcrumb

# Point-of-Use Reverse Osmosis Systems

![Certification systems banner](/sites/default/files/2017-02/ws-specs-certificationsystems.jpg)
![](/system/files/styles/large/private/images/2021-07/ws-button-search-products.png?itok=_OH2pce5)

A point-of-use reverse osmosis (RO) system is a water filtration device that is connected to a single fixture (e.g., under the kitchen sink) and uses the process of RO to remove contaminants from the water supplied to that fixture. RO is the process by which pressure forces water through a semi-permeable membrane, creating a stream of treated water, called “permeate,” and a stream of reject water called “concentrate” or “brine.” These systems can potentially remove water contaminants such as lead, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), PFAS, arsenic, bacteria, and viruses. Point-of-use RO systems are typically installed in residential settings but can also be found in commercial office spaces or kitchens.

**On this Page**

![A point-of-use reverse osmosis (RO) system.](/system/files/styles/medium/private/images/2022-01/ws-ro-systems.jpg?itok=qzqPwyu3)

**Treat With Efficiency**

While RO systems can improve water quality, these systems can also generate a significant amount of water waste to operate. For example, a typical point-of-use RO system will generate five gallons or more of reject water for every gallon of treated water produced. Some inefficient units will generate up to 10 gallons of reject water for every gallon of treated water produced. In contrast, point-of-use RO systems that earn the WaterSense label must demonstrate that they send just 2.3 gallons of water or less down the drain for every gallon of treated water they produce.

All products bearing the WaterSense label are independently certified to ensure they meet EPA criteria for efficiency and performance. The WaterSense specification for point-of-use RO systems ensures that labeled RO systems are just as effective at providing the high-quality treated water consumers expect from these products. For more information on RO systems, including how they work and how to select an RO system that is right for you, view this WaterSense report, 

[Treating Water With Less Waste (pdf)](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-11/ws-products-ro-systems-mini-report.pdf)
(11.2 MB)
.

While the water provided by public water suppliers in the United States is some of the safest in the world, there are times when consumers may want to employ additional treatment technology to ensure safety of well water or publicly-supplied water. Due to the water-intensive nature of RO, WaterSense does not intend to promote the installation of RO systems for all applications or encourage their use over other water treatment technologies that do not waste as much water (e.g., filtration systems). In some cases, other treatment methods that use little or no water, such as filtration, are sufficient to meet consumer treatment needs. For more information, read the [WaterSense Guide to Selecting Water Treatment Systems (pdf)](/system/files/documents/2025-01/ws-products-home-water-treatment-guide_v2_508.pdf).

**WaterSense Savings**

For consumers who are planning to replace their reverse osmosis system at the point of water use, selecting a WaterSense labeled model will reduce water use on average by more than 3,100 gallons of water per year compared to the water usage of a typical point-of-use RO system. This translates to a savings of 47,000 gallons over the lifetime of the system.

If all point-of-use RO systems sold in the United States were WaterSense labeled, we could save more than 3.1 billion gallons of water across the country annually, which is equivalent to the annual household water needs of nearly 41,000 American homes.

Read the 

[Point-of-Use Reverse Osmosis Systems fact sheet (pdf)](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-11/ws-products-ro-systems-factsheet.pdf)
(1.3 MB)
for more information.

**Performance**

As part of the WaterSense specification development process, EPA examined key performance metrics to ensure consumer satisfaction. EPA also considered the impact of reducing water waste on RO system performance.

All WaterSense labeled point-of-use RO systems are independently certified to meet performance criteria to ensure user satisfaction and health and safety. Performance criteria address the following areas:

See the Performance Overview for more information on how WaterSense assessed performance when developing the specification.

**Specification**

In November 2024, EPA released the *WaterSense Specification for Point-of-Use Reverse Osmosis Systems*.

EPA hosted a meeting for manufacturers to discuss the final specification and process for earning the WaterSense label for RO systems on December 17, 2024.  The presentation and recording are available below.

For more information about the specification development process for point-of-use RO systems, including the draft specification, public response to the draft specification, and EPA’s response to public comments, please visit the [Specification for Point-of-Use Reverse Osmosis Systems Background Materials Page](/watersense/product-background-materials).

Manufacturers that produce point-of-use RO systems meeting EPA’s efficiency and performance criteria can apply to have their products certified to earn the WaterSense label. Before submitting products for testing, manufacturers must have a partnership agreement with EPA in place. Visit the [manufacturer section on the WaterSense partner page](/watersense/join-watersense#Manufacturers) to learn more.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Reverse osmosis membranes possess the smallest pore sizes, typically around 0.0001 micrometers, effectively filtering out contaminants, minerals, and microorganisms from water.
RO technology excels at removing dissolved solids and salts, ensuring high-quality water suitable for various applications, including drinking and industrial processes.
Reverse osmosis is crucial for potable water production, especially in water-scarce regions, providing a reliable method for desalinating seawater and purifying brackish water.

RO technology is widely utilized in industries such as pharmaceuticals, food and beverage, and semiconductor manufacturing, highlighting its significance in ensuring water quality and process efficiency.
